qa_pairs.question: How do I establish a secure SSH tunnel to a VNC server running on a compute node on the Nibi cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: For Nibi, the tunnel command requires using `-J username@nibi.alliancecan.ca` and specifying `localhost` for the remote host. The format is `ssh -J username@nibi.alliancecan.ca -L localforwardedport:localhost:remotelisteningport computenode`. For example, if your VNC server is on compute node `c48` listening on port `5901`, and you want to forward local port `5905`, the command would be `ssh -J username@nibi.alliancecan.ca -L 5905:localhost:5901 c48`. This approach also requires an SSH key pair to be created on your laptop, with the public key added to your `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` file on Nibi.

qa_pairs.question: Once an SSH tunnel is established, how do I connect to the VNC server from my local desktop using TigerVNC Viewer, and what kind of desktop environment will I experience?
qa_pairs.answer: If you are on a Linux desktop, open a new local terminal window and use the command `vncviewer localhost:port`, for example, `vncviewer localhost:5905`. For Mac or Windows desktops, click the TigerVNC Viewer application icon and enter `localhost:port` in the "VNC viewer: Connection details" dialogue box that appears. After successfully connecting, you will be presented with a Linux MATE desktop. From this desktop, you can launch a terminal by navigating to "Applications -> System Tools -> MATE Terminal" and then invoke programs like `xclock` or MATLAB (after loading its module).

qa_pairs.question: What are the methods for managing the VNC server password and terminating a running VNC server on an Alliance cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: To reset your VNC server password, use the `vncpasswd` command, which will prompt you to enter and verify a new password. Optionally, you can completely remove your VNC configuration, including the password, by deleting your `~/.vnc` directory; the next time you run `vncserver`, you will be prompted to set a new password. To terminate a specific running VNC server, use `vncserver -kill :DISPLAY#`, replacing `DISPLAY#` with the session's display number (e.g., `vncserver -kill :44`). To instantly terminate all VNC servers running under your username on a node, you can use the command `pkill Xvnc -u $USER`.
